question_text: Be the change you want to see in others - Gandhiji.

points.text: Gandhi's call for self-transformation as prerequisite for social change; contrast between preaching and practicing
Individual responsibility over blaming systems – corruption, littering, gender discrimination begin with personal choices
Examples of change-makers: Gandhi himself (Dandi March, charkha), Kiran Bedi (prison reforms), social entrepreneurs
Ripple effect: one person's ethical conduct influences family, workplace, community (Swachh Bharat, anti-bribery movements)
Psychological basis: cognitive dissonance, role modeling, social proof – leaders as catalysts
Counter-argument: systemic issues need structural change; but Gandhi insists individual change enables collective action
Becoming the change is the most powerful form of leadership; sustainable transformation begins within

question_text: Is the Colonial mentality hindering India's success?

points.text: Colonial mentality – psychological legacy of British rule: preference for Western over indigenous, servility to authority, inferiority complex
Manifestations in education: English obsession, Macaulay's system still dominant, neglect of Indian languages and knowledge systems
In governance: bureaucratic hierarchy (steel frame), red-tapism, IAS as 'collector Raj' – remnants of colonial administration
In economy: preference for foreign brands (wheatish complexion bias), undervaluation of indigenous products (khadi, millets, traditional medicine)
Hindrance to success: stifles innovation (fear of failure), limits self-reliance (Atmanirbhar hampered), perpetuates brain drain
Positive counters: rising pride in Indian achievements (ISRO, digital public infra, startup ecosystem), cultural revival (yoga, Ayurveda), New Education Policy 2020
Colonial mentality still present but diminishing; full success requires decolonizing the mind while selectively embracing global best practices

question_text: GDP (Gross Domestic Product) along with GDH (Gross Domestic Happiness) would be the right indices for judging the well-being of a country.

points.text: Limitations of GDP: ignores distribution, environment, health, leisure, social connections – Kuznets (1934) warning
GDH concept from Bhutan: nine domains (psychological well-being, health, education, time use, culture, governance, community vitality, environment, living standards)
Why GDP+GDH is superior: captures economic output + quality of life; addresses Easterlin paradox (happiness doesn't rise linearly with income)
Global examples: UN World Happiness Report, OECD Better Life Index, India's efforts (Ease of Living Index, NITI Aayog's SDG index)
Challenges: GDH measurement subjective, cross-cultural comparability difficult, political manipulation risk, trade-offs between growth and happiness
India's context: high GDP growth but low happiness rankings (India 136/146 in World Happiness 2022); need for mental health, work-life balance, environmental sustainability
Neither GDP alone nor GDH alone is sufficient; composite indices combining economic, social, and environmental metrics are the way forward

question_text: Science and Technology is the panacea for the growth and security of the nation.

points.text: Panacea means universal remedy – S&T has transformed lives but cannot solve all problems alone
Growth through S&T: Green Revolution, IT revolution (India as software hub), space technology (ISRO's commercial launches), biotech (vaccines, GM crops)
Security through S&T: nuclear deterrent, missile systems (Agni, Prithvi), cyber security, surveillance (satellites, drones), border fencing technology
Limitations: S&T cannot solve social issues (caste, gender, communalism), ethical dilemmas (AI, genetic engineering), distributional inequalities (digital divide)
Potential harms: cyber warfare, autonomous weapons, job displacement, environmental damage (e-waste, nuclear waste)
Need for balanced approach: S&T must be guided by ethics, inclusive policies, regulatory frameworks (Personal Data Protection Bill, AI ethics guidelines)
S&T is necessary but not sufficient; growth and security also require good governance, social justice, and human values
